title: Quantitative decompositions of Lipschitz mappings
speaker: Guy C. David
abstract: Given a Lipschitz map, it is often useful to chop the domain into pieces on which the map has simple behavior. For example, depending on the dimensions of source and target, one may ask for pieces on which the map behaves like a bi-Lipschitz embedding or like a linear projection. For many issues, it is even more useful if this decomposition is quantitative, i.e., with bounds independent of the particular map or spaces involved.

proof_stages.label: Full-dimensional quantitative-decomposition template
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines quantitative decompositions of Lipschitz maps, reviews the Federer nonquantitative and Shul quantitative full-dimensional bi-Lipschitz decomposition results, and fixes Euclidean domains as the setting where arbitrary metric targets remain possible.

proof_stages.label: Hard Sard sets for dimension-lowering maps
proof_stages.summary: Since bi-Lipschitz pieces are impossible for maps from R^{n+m} to n-dimensional targets, the appropriate simple pieces are defined as hard Sard sets, where after a global bi-Lipschitz coordinate change the map behaves like an orthogonal projection.

proof_stages.label: Kaufman obstruction to image-content garbage
proof_stages.summary: Kaufman’s C^1 surjection from the 3-cube to the 2-square with derivative of rank at most one shows that positive image content need not produce any hard Sard pieces, so the naive analogue of Shul’s theorem is false.

proof_stages.label: Mapping content and corrected theorem
proof_stages.summary: The corrected error notion is the Azzam–Schul mapping content, which detects either small domain measure or strong n-dimensional compression, and the main theorem asserts a quantitative hard-Sard decomposition with garbage small in this mapping content.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to supplemented bi-Lipschitz pieces
proof_stages.summary: The proof reduces the hard-Sard decomposition to finding pieces, after controlled coordinate changes, on which the augmented map (x,y)↦(f(x,y),y) is bi-Lipschitz, with the leftover set still small in mapping content.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative differentiation and cube trichotomy
proof_stages.summary: Azzam–Schul quantitative differentiation gives a packing bound for cubes on which f is not approximately linear, while low-rank approximately linear cubes are absorbed into mapping-content garbage, leaving good cubes where f is close to a rank-n linear map.

proof_stages.label: Controlled switching and clockwork rotations
proof_stages.summary: On good cubes the proof controls how often the approximating rank-n directions can switch along dyadic chains, and then assembles local rotations at those boundedly many scales into the coordinate changes that make the supplemented maps bi-Lipschitz.

examples.name: Federer-style nonquantitative bi-Lipschitz decomposition
examples.note: The classical Euclidean Lipschitz-map decomposition illustrates the general decomposition template while emphasizing what is missing quantitatively: controlled constants and a finite controlled number of pieces.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the Federer-style decomposition as a running illustrative case, identifies the garbage set and simplicity condition, and sketches how Rademacher’s theorem produces the pieces via approximately constant derivatives

examples.name: Carnot groups and metric-domain failures
examples.note: The discussion of Carnot-group positive results and Semmes/Laakso/Li–Rajala counterexamples situates the Euclidean-domain hypothesis by showing that analogous decompositions can fail in general metric domains.
examples.engagement_reason: Carnot groups, Heisenberg examples, and metric-domain counterexamples are cited as background positive and negative results without working through any construction or verification

examples.name: Planar hard-Sard-set picture
examples.note: The n=m=1 drawing with blue fibers and red transverse curves makes the hard Sard definition visually concrete as vertical constancy and horizontal bi-Lipschitz behavior after a coordinate change.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws and reasons through the planar hard-Sard-set picture, tracing fibers and transverse curves and how a bi-Lipschitz coordinate change straightens them into vertical and horizontal directions

examples.name: Distance-to-a-point cone function
examples.note: An audience question about a distance function/cone tests quantitative differentiation, and the response explains that the bad cubes containing the cone point have summable total volume.
examples.engagement_reason: in response to a question, the speaker checks the cone distance-to-a-point example against quantitative differentiation by identifying the bad cubes as those containing the origin and explaining their volumes form a controlled geometric sum
